title: Why You Dissociate in Therapy
researcherTopic: somatic experiencing, completing the stress response
metaDescription: Understand why you dissociate in therapy. Learn how this protective mechanism works and how to gently return to presence by building a felt sense of safety.
imageDescription: A delicate ceramic bowl, repaired with shimmering gold kintsugi, rests on a rustic wooden surface. Warm, gentle morning light streams in, illuminating the golden cracks and casting a soft glow on the scene, symbolizing healing and beautiful imperfection.
htmlContent:
I've sat across from hundreds of people who describe the same bewildering phenomenon... the moment in a therapy session when the world seems to recede, the therapist's voice becomes a distant hum, and a thick, insulating fog rolls in. It’s a significant sense of disappearing while sitting perfectly still, a silent exit from a room you haven’t physically left. We call it dissociation, but it’s more accurately described as the body’s last-ditch effort to protect the psyche from what it believes will be an annihilating experience. It is proof of survival, not a sign of being broken.

The Silence That Isn't Empty: Dissociation as a Survival Signal

We often misunderstand the nature of the silence that descends during a dissociative episode, viewing it as a void or an absence of experience. Yet, it is anything but empty. This state is a highly sophisticated, biologically programmed survival strategy, a form of deep retreat when the more commonly known fight or flight responses are deemed unavailable or ineffective. The body, in its significant and sometimes archaic wisdom, chooses to shut down sensation rather than risk re-experiencing an overwhelming flood of terror, shame, or grief. It is a physiological state of suspended animation, where the thinking mind goes offline to preserve the integrity of the whole.

This response is not a cognitive error; it is the activation of the most primitive part of our vagus nerve, the dorsal vagal complex, which governs our immobilization and shutdown responses. Think of it as an animal playing dead to survive an attack - a brilliant strategy in the wild, but a strikingly confusing one in a therapist's office. The system isn’t making a mistake; it’s running an old software program that once ensured survival in an environment where connection was dangerous and stillness was the only safety. The dissociation was the only way her system knew to avoid shattering. It’s a protective mechanism so deeply ingrained that it operates far beneath the threshold of conscious thought, a silent testament to the body’s unwavering commitment to keeping you alive, even if it means you aren’t fully present to live.

The Body's Ancient Logic: Why Safety Precedes Revelation

The therapeutic process is largely built on the premise of revelation - the uncovering of memories, the speaking of truths, the expression of buried emotions. But the body operates on a much older and more fundamental premise: safety. Before there can be any meaningful revelation, there must first be a deeply felt, somatically registered sense of safety. When we dissociate in a session, the body is communicating a vital piece of information: it does not feel safe enough to proceed. The environment may be objectively safe ~ a quiet room, a compassionate therapist ~ but the nervous system’s threat detection system, or neuroception, is picking up on subtle cues that it associates with past danger.

The Therapist’s Chair as a Perceived Threat

The very architecture of traditional talk therapy can, for many survivors of complex trauma, become an unconscious reenactment of past dynamics that necessitated dissociation in the first place. Consider the setup: one person is in a position of perceived authority, holding knowledge and asking questions, while the other is expected to be vulnerable, to reveal, to perform emotionality in a way that is legible and coherent. The simple act of being observed so intently can trigger a striking sense of exposure and the accompanying fear of getting it wrong, of being too much or not enough. The body remembers the danger of being seen and found wanting.

A therapy fidget that many find soothing for restless hands is Tangle Therapy Relax (paid link).

Beyond the Freeze: Titrating the Return to Presence

The process requires us to respect the intelligence of the freeze. Instead of trying to force our way out of it, we get curious about it. What does the numbness feel like? Where does it begin in the body? By bringing a gentle, non-demanding attention to the state itself, we begin to rob it of its power. We are no longer a victim of the state; we are its witness. This shift in relationship is the beginning of everything. It is how we learn to self-regulate not by controlling our experience, but by becoming intimate with it.

The Architecture of Embodied Awareness

This process is deeply personal and cannot be rushed. It involves discovering what, specifically, helps your unique nervous system feel more regulated. For one person, it might be the gentle pressure of a hand on their own heart. There is no universal prescription. It is a deeply intimate inquiry into the language of your own body, learning what it needs to feel anchored in the present moment. That becomes the real work of healing ~ not the dramatic catharsis, but the quiet, consistent, and deeply compassionate practice of building a home within yourself, a place where your body is not the enemy.

When the Story Is Not the Point

In a culture obsessed with narrative, we have come to believe that healing is synonymous with telling our story. We believe that if we can just find the right words, arrange the events in the correct order, and have it witnessed by another, we will be set free. And while there is obvious power in being heard and validated, for many who have endured complex trauma, the relentless focus on the story can become a trap. The mind gets caught in the "why" and the "what," while the body remains frozen in the "now" of a past event. Dissociation in therapy is often a signal that the system has had enough of the story; it is pleading for a different kind of engagement.

That becomes why somatic modalities are so crucial in the treatment of complex trauma. They bypass the exhausted thinking mind and speak directly to the body in its own language... the language of sensation, impulse, and movement. They create the conditions for this bound survival energy to be gently and safely discharged. When this happens, the nervous system can finally stand down from high alert. The story might make more sense afterward, or it might become irrelevant. The point is that the charge is gone. The body has returned to a state of equilibrium. And in that quiet space, a new kind of story can begin to emerge ~ one that is not about what happened, but about who we are in the aftermath.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link).

A Compassionate Return

The path out of the dissociative fog is not a straight line, nor is it a battle to be won. It is a gentle, often meandering, return to oneself. It requires a radical shift in our relationship with these protective states ~ viewing them not as pathologies to be eliminated, but as intelligent, albeit outdated, strategies that have ensured our survival. What we find here is not a process of forcing or fixing, but of listening and befriending. We learn to meet the numbness with warmth, the emptiness with curiosity, and the fear with a steady, unwavering compassion.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is dissociating in therapy a sign that I have the wrong therapist?

Not necessarily. While a poor therapeutic fit can certainly contribute to feelings of unsafety, dissociation is more often a reflection of your own nervous system's programming than a failing on the therapist's part. The key is whether your therapist recognizes this, responds with compassion and skill, and is willing to shift the approach to prioritize somatic safety over cognitive insight. A good therapist will see your dissociation not as resistance, but as crucial information guiding the process.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link).

I know, I know.

If I stop dissociating, will I be flooded with painful memories?

What we find here is a common and understandable fear. A skilled, trauma-informed therapist will not allow you to be flooded. The entire process is built around the principle of titration... The goal is to build your capacity to tolerate sensation gradually, not to overwhelm you. You will always be in control of the pace and depth of the work.

How can I feel safer in my body when it feels like the source of the problem?

The work back to the body is a slow one, and it begins with finding "islands of safety" or neutral sensations. You don't have to love your body or even feel comfortable in it at first. The practice is to find one small part that doesn't feel bad... By placing your attention on these neutral places, you begin to teach your brain that it's possible to inhabit your body without being consumed by pain or fear. It's a practice of patience and micro-steps, as explored in our guide to polyvagal theory.

Can I ever fully stop dissociating?

The goal is not necessarily to eliminate dissociation entirely, as it remains a natural human coping mechanism. The goal is to reduce its frequency and intensity, and to have more choice in the matter. Instead, you develop a wider range of coping strategies and a greater capacity to stay present, even when things are difficult. Dissociation may shift from being a debilitating daily reality to a rare signal that you are far outside your window of tolerance and need to practice compassionate self-regulation.

For brain health and mood support, a well-regarded option is Nordic Naturals Ultimate Omega (paid link).

The return from the fog of dissociation is not a grand, cinematic moment of arrival, but a series of small, quiet awakenings. It is the gradual discovery that the body is not a minefield but a field, that sensation is not a threat but a language, and that presence is not a state to be achieved but a home to which we can always return. You don't arrive at peace; you stop walking away from it.
